news_summary: Recent advancements reveal promising progress in women's cancer prevention, potential human regenerative abilities, innovative non-surgical arthritis treatments, and improved AI-driven disease management.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes révèlent des progrès prometteurs dans la prévention du cancer chez les femmes, les capacités régénératives potentielles de l'organisme humain, les traitements innovants et non chirurgicaux de l'arthrite, ainsi que l'amélioration de la prise en charge des maladies grâce à l'intelligence artificielle.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances revelan progresos prometedores en la prevención del cáncer en las mujeres, las posibles capacidades regenerativas del ser humano, tratamientos innovadores y no quirúrgicos para la artritis y una mejor gestión de las enfermedades gracias a la inteligencia artificial.

articles.title: No young women have died of cervical cancer in England for years
articles.summary: We already know the vaccine against human papillomavirus, or HPV, greatly reduces infections and cases of cervical cancer, and now we have the first evidence it prevents deaths too
articles.content:
We already know the vaccine against human papillomavirus, or HPV, greatly reduces infections and cases of cervical cancer, and now we have the first evidence it prevents deaths too

HPV vaccines have revolutionised cervical-cancer prevention since the first one became available in 2006 Subaas Shrestha/NurPhoto via Getty Images

No women in England aged 20 to 24 died of cervical cancer between 2020 and 2024. This is the first time that zero cervical-cancer deaths have been recorded for this age group, and it’s thanks to the introduction of a vaccine against the human papillomavirus, or HPV.

“The results are stunning,” says Peter Sasieni at Queen Mary University of London. “It’s an awful thing when somebody dies very young from cervical cancer. This is a real triumph for vaccination, a real triumph for science and a real triumph for public health to get that vaccine out there with very high uptake very rapidly.”

Advertisement

HPV is spread by several kinds of sexual activity, and many strains genetically modify cells in a way that is extremely likely to cause cancer. Women can get cervical cancer in their twenties because of the virus, and around the world many are still dying because of it.

The first HPV vaccine became available in 2006. In the UK, it has been offered to girls aged 12 or 13 since 2008. It’s been offered to boys since 2019, both to protect them from other HPV-triggered cancers – like those affecting the mouth, anus, throat and penis – and to prevent them from infecting others.

The study is the first evidence that the HPV vaccine prevents cervical-cancer-related deaths, in addition to dramatically reducing HPV infections and the incidence of cervical cancers. It may seem obvious that it prevents deaths, but we know that the women who are least likely to get vaccinated are also the least likely to go for screening, says Sasieni. So, there was a worry that the vaccine has mainly been preventing cancers that would have been detected early by screening and successfully treated, but not preventing those that would get missed by screening and therefore be more likely to kill women.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

Fortunately, this doesn’t appear to be the case. Sasieni and his colleague Milena Falcaro, also at Queen Mary, have been monitoring cervical-cancer rates and deaths in England, and noticed that between 2020 and 2024 – the most recent data available – there were no deaths among women aged 20 to 24. Based on historical rates, around 23 deaths would be expected. “As far back as I’ve seen data, there’s never been a year with none, and so five years in a row with no deaths is really quite something,” says Sasieni.

The dramatic drop is almost certainly due to the HPV vaccine – around 90 per cent of women in England aged 20 to 24 were vaccinated when they were 12 or 13. “This hugely encouraging news shows the life-saving impact of the HPV vaccine, and it’s incredibly exciting to be able to say to this whole generation: cervical cancer and some other cancers shouldn’t be a risk for you,” says Caroline Temmink, director of vaccination for the National Health Service (NHS) in England.

While this study looked only at cervical cancer, the benefits of this vaccine also apply to other HPV-related cancers. It should also protect against warts on the skin and genital and anal areas in both sexes.

Sasieni and Falcaro note that there were some deaths among women aged 25 to 29, but far fewer than would be expected. Altogether, they estimate around 200 lives have already been saved, and this is just the start. “The 200 which we’ve estimated in the paper is really just the tip of the iceberg, because it looks like there’s long-term protection against infection,” says Sasieni. “In the future, 18,000 deaths might be a rough estimate of what we’re preventing so far.”

Worldwide, however, uptake of the HPV vaccine is low and the rate of cervical cancer is still rising. Death rates may also rise in the UK because fewer teenagers are getting vaccinated. “The bad news is that the vaccine uptake has fallen quite dramatically since covid,” says Sasieni.

“Alongside cervical screening, HPV vaccination is central to the NHS ambition to eliminate cervical cancer by 2040,” says Temmink. “It’s a safe and effective vaccine and we urge everyone eligible to take up the offer when invited.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant public health achievement: zero cervical cancer deaths among young women in England due to high HPV vaccine uptake. This represents a major breakthrough in cancer prevention with broad societal impact and clear evidence of life-saving benefits.
articles.personality_title: No young women in England died of cervical cancer thanks to HPV vaccine
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cervical cancer is caused by the human papillomavirus (HPV), which spreads through sexual activity. Since 2006, vaccines have been available to protect people from HPV infections and reduce cervical cancer cases.

**What happened** – Between 2020 and 2024, no women aged 20 to 24 in England died from cervical cancer. This is the first time zero deaths have been recorded in this age group. Researchers link this success to the HPV vaccine given to about 90% of girls in that age group when they were 12 or 13.

**Impact** – This is the first clear proof that the HPV vaccine not only lowers cancer cases but also prevents deaths. It shows the vaccine works even for women who might miss early cancer screenings. Experts estimate that around 200 lives have already been saved in England, with many more expected in the future.

**What's next step** – The NHS aims to eliminate cervical cancer by 2040 through vaccination and screening. However, fewer teenagers have been getting the vaccine since COVID-19, so health officials encourage everyone eligible to get vaccinated to keep this progress going.

**One-sentence takeaway** – High HPV vaccine use in England has led to zero cervical cancer deaths among young women for the first time, proving the vaccine’s life-saving power.
articles.personality_title_fr: Aucune jeune femme en Angleterre n’est morte du cancer du col grâce au vaccin contre le VPH
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le cancer du col de l’utérus est causé par le virus du papillome humain (VPH), qui se transmet lors de rapports sexuels. Depuis 2006, des vaccins protègent contre les infections à VPH et réduisent les cas de ce cancer.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Entre 2020 et 2024, aucune femme âgée de 20 à 24 ans en Angleterre n’est morte du cancer du col. C’est la première fois que ce chiffre est nul. Les chercheurs attribuent ce succès au vaccin contre le VPH, reçu par environ 90 % des filles de cet âge lorsqu’elles avaient 12 ou 13 ans.

**Impact** – C’est la première preuve claire que le vaccin contre le VPH réduit non seulement les cas de cancer, mais empêche aussi les décès. Il protège même les femmes qui ne participent pas aux dépistages précoces. Les experts estiment qu’environ 200 vies ont déjà été sauvées en Angleterre, et beaucoup d’autres le seront.

**Prochaine étape** – Le NHS vise à éliminer le cancer du col d’ici 2040 grâce à la vaccination et au dépistage. Cependant, la vaccination a baissé depuis la pandémie de COVID-19, donc les autorités sanitaires encouragent toutes les personnes éligibles à se faire vacciner pour maintenir ces progrès.

**Phrase clé** – Une forte vaccination contre le VPH en Angleterre a permis pour la première fois d’avoir zéro décès par cancer du col chez les jeunes femmes, prouvant l’efficacité vitale du vaccin.
articles.personality_title_es: Ninguna mujer joven en Inglaterra murió de cáncer de cuello uterino gracias a la vacuna contra el VPH
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El cáncer de cuello uterino es causado por el virus del papiloma humano (VPH), que se transmite por actividad sexual. Desde 2006, hay vacunas que protegen contra las infecciones por VPH y reducen los casos de este cáncer.

**Qué pasó** – Entre 2020 y 2024, ninguna mujer de 20 a 24 años en Inglaterra murió por cáncer de cuello uterino. Es la primera vez que se registra cero muertes en este grupo. Los investigadores atribuyen este logro a la vacuna contra el VPH, que recibió cerca del 90 % de las chicas cuando tenían 12 o 13 años.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera prueba clara de que la vacuna no solo reduce los casos de cáncer, sino que también previene muertes. Protege incluso a quienes no se realizan exámenes tempranos. Se calcula que ya se han salvado unas 200 vidas en Inglaterra y se esperan muchas más.

**Próximo paso** – El NHS quiere eliminar el cáncer de cuello uterino para 2040 mediante vacunación y exámenes. Pero la vacunación ha bajado desde la COVID-19, por lo que las autoridades de salud animan a todos los que puedan a vacunarse para mantener este avance.

**Frase clave** – El alto uso de la vacuna contra el VPH en Inglaterra ha llevado a cero muertes por cáncer de cuello uterino en mujeres jóvenes por primera vez, demostrando el poder salvavidas de la vacuna.
articles.image_prompt: A gentle, detailed painting of a vibrant, protective shield made of delicate, interlocking flowers shaped like cervical cells, glowing softly around a group of young female silhouettes represented as blooming buds, all set against a calm, natural background of soft blues and greens symbolizing health and hope.

articles.title: Humans may have hidden regenerative powers
articles.summary: Scientists have taken a surprising step toward unlocking regeneration in mammals, showing that the ability to rebuild complex body parts may not be lost after all—it may simply be switched off. Using a two-stage treatment, researchers redirected the body’s normal healing response away from scar formation and toward regrowth, successfully restoring bone, joints, ligaments, and tendons after amputation in animal studies.
articles.content:
For generations, scientists have viewed the inability to regrow lost body parts as one of the fundamental limitations of humans and other mammals. While creatures such as salamanders can regenerate entire limbs, humans typically heal injuries by forming scar tissue.

New research from the Texas A&M College of Veterinary Medicine and Biomedical Sciences (VMBS), however, suggests that regenerative abilities may not be entirely absent in mammals. Instead, they could be hidden within the body's normal healing machinery, waiting to be activated under the right conditions.

"Why some animals can regenerate and others, particularly humans, can't is a big question that has been asked since Aristotle," said Dr. Ken Muneoka, a professor in the VMBS' Department of Veterinary Physiology & Pharmacology (VTPP). "I've spent my career trying to understand that."

In a study published in Nature Communications, Muneoka and colleagues describe a new two-step treatment that enabled the regeneration of bone, joint structures, and ligaments. Although the regrown tissues were not perfect replicas of the originals, the researchers believe the approach could eventually help reduce scarring and improve tissue repair after amputations.

Redirecting Healing Away From Scar Formation

When mammals are injured, the body usually responds with fibrosis. During this process, fibroblast cells quickly close the wound and create scar tissue. While this response helps prevent infection and further damage, it also limits the body's ability to rebuild what was lost.

Animals capable of regeneration follow a different path. In salamanders, for example, similar cells gather into a structure called a blastema, which serves as a foundation for new tissue growth.

"It's as if these cells can move in two different directions," Muneoka said. "They could either make a scar or make a blastema. Our research focused on redirecting the behavior of fibroblasts already present at the injury site."

To explore whether mammalian healing could be pushed toward regeneration, the research team developed a treatment that uses two well-known growth factors in sequence.

The first step involved applying fibroblast growth factor 2 (FGF2) after the wound had already healed over. By waiting until the initial healing process was complete, the researchers allowed the body to respond normally before intervening.

According to Muneoka, the team then "changed what happens next."

FGF2 encouraged the formation of a blastema-like structure, something that does not typically occur in mammals after this type of injury. Several days later, the researchers applied a second growth factor, bone morphogenetic protein 2 (BMP2), which prompted those cells to begin building new tissues.

"This is really a two-step process," Muneoka said. "You first shift the cells away from scarring, and then you provide the signals that tell them what to build."

Rethinking the Role of Stem Cells

One of the study's most important findings is that regeneration may not require adding stem cells from outside the body, an approach commonly explored in regenerative medicine.

"You don't have to actually get stem cells and put them back in," Muneoka said. "They're already there -- you just need to learn how to get them to behave the way you want."

Dr. Larry Suva, another VTPP professor involved in the study, said the results challenge long-standing assumptions about what mammalian cells are capable of doing.

"The cells that we thought to be unprogrammable, in fact are," Suva said. "The capacity is not absent -- it's just obscured."

The researchers also found evidence that cells can be redirected to create structures outside their usual location. This process, known as positional re-specification, is an important part of development.

In practical terms, cells that would normally help form one type of tissue can be instructed to rebuild a different structure following an injury.

Regrowing Bone, Tendons, Ligaments, and Joints

Although the regenerated tissues were not exact matches to the original anatomy, the researchers successfully restored all of the major structures that had been removed during amputation, including bone, tendon, ligament, and joint tissue.

The regenerated areas contained both skeletal components and connective tissues arranged in patterns resembling natural anatomy.

"We regenerated what you would expect to see at that level of injury," Muneoka said. "The structures are there -- just not in a perfect form."

The findings also suggest that regeneration depends on multiple biological pathways working together. Rebuilding tissue appears to be far more complex than activating a single mechanism.

Potential Benefits for Wound Healing

While the research remains in its early stages, the scientists believe it could have practical applications long before complete regeneration becomes possible.

Rather than focusing solely on replacing missing structures, the approach may help improve healing outcomes by reducing scar formation and enhancing tissue repair.

"People should start thinking about using these signals during the healing process," Muneoka said. "Even shifting the response slightly away from scarring could have real benefits."

The path toward clinical testing may also be more straightforward than with many experimental therapies. BMP2 already has FDA approval for certain medical applications, and FGF2 is currently being evaluated in multiple clinical trials.

A New View of Mammalian Regeneration

The study adds to growing evidence that regeneration in mammals may not be a completely lost trait. Instead, it may be a dormant capability that normally remains inactive during healing.

"This changes the way we think about what's possible," Suva said. "Once you show that regeneration can be activated, it opens the door to asking entirely new questions."

For Muneoka, those questions have driven decades of research and now have a promising new framework.

"Regenerative failure in mammals can be rescued," he said. "Now we have a model to begin figuring out how."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in regenerative medicine showing that mammals, including humans, may have latent regenerative abilities that can be activated to regrow complex tissues after injury. This discovery has broad implications for improving healing and reducing scarring in millions of people, representing a meaningful advancement in health science with potential large-scale benefits.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find hidden way to help mammals regrow lost body parts
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For a long time, scientists believed mammals like humans cannot regrow lost body parts. Unlike animals such as salamanders, which can regenerate entire limbs, mammals usually heal injuries by forming scar tissue, which stops new tissue from growing.

**What happened** – Researchers at Texas A&M developed a two-step treatment that changes how the body heals after an injury. First, they used a growth factor called FGF2 to stop the usual scar-forming process. Then, they applied another growth factor, BMP2, which encouraged cells to rebuild bones, joints, ligaments, and tendons in animals after amputation.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that mammals might already have the ability to regrow tissues, but it is normally turned off. The treatment worked by guiding the body’s own cells without needing to add new stem cells. Although the new tissues were not perfect copies, the study proves it is possible to reduce scarring and improve healing in ways not seen before.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to explore how to make this treatment better and safer for humans. Since BMP2 is already approved for some medical uses and FGF2 is in clinical trials, testing this approach on people could happen sooner than expected. The research also opens new questions about how to control healing and regeneration in mammals.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers found a way to switch on hidden regenerative powers in mammals, allowing damaged body parts to grow back instead of forming scars.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent comment aider les mammifères à régénérer des parties du corps perdues
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant longtemps, les scientifiques ont pensé que les mammifères, comme les humains, ne pouvaient pas régénérer des parties perdues de leur corps. Contrairement aux salamandres, capables de repousser un membre entier, les mammifères guérissent généralement par la formation de cicatrices, ce qui empêche la croissance de nouveaux tissus.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs du Texas A&M ont mis au point un traitement en deux étapes qui modifie la façon dont le corps guérit après une blessure. D'abord, ils ont utilisé un facteur de croissance appelé FGF2 pour arrêter la formation habituelle de cicatrices. Ensuite, ils ont appliqué un autre facteur, BMP2, qui a encouragé les cellules à reconstruire os, articulations, ligaments et tendons chez des animaux après une amputation.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les mammifères pourraient déjà avoir la capacité de régénérer des tissus, mais que cette capacité est normalement désactivée. Le traitement a fonctionné en guidant les cellules du corps sans avoir besoin d'ajouter de nouvelles cellules souches. Bien que les nouveaux tissus ne soient pas des copies parfaites, l'étude prouve qu'il est possible de réduire les cicatrices et d'améliorer la guérison d'une manière inédite.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient d'améliorer et de sécuriser ce traitement pour les humains. Puisque BMP2 est déjà approuvé pour certaines utilisations médicales et que FGF2 est en essais cliniques, ce traitement pourrait être testé chez l'homme plus rapidement que prévu. La recherche ouvre aussi de nouvelles questions sur la façon de contrôler la guérison et la régénération chez les mammifères.

**Phrase clé** – Des chercheurs ont trouvé un moyen d'activer des pouvoirs régénérateurs cachés chez les mammifères, permettant à des parties du corps endommagées de repousser au lieu de former des cicatrices.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren cómo ayudar a los mamíferos a regenerar partes del cuerpo perdidas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos pensaron que los mamíferos, como los humanos, no podían regenerar partes perdidas de su cuerpo. A diferencia de los salamandras, que pueden volver a crecer una extremidad completa, los mamíferos suelen sanar las heridas formando cicatrices, lo que impide que crezcan nuevos tejidos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Texas A&M desarrollaron un tratamiento en dos pasos que cambia cómo el cuerpo sana después de una lesión. Primero usaron un factor de crecimiento llamado FGF2 para detener la formación normal de cicatrices. Luego aplicaron otro factor, BMP2, que animó a las células a reconstruir huesos, articulaciones, ligamentos y tendones en animales después de una amputación.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que los mamíferos podrían tener la capacidad de regenerar tejidos, pero esta capacidad está normalmente apagada. El tratamiento funcionó al guiar las propias células del cuerpo sin necesidad de agregar células madre nuevas. Aunque los tejidos nuevos no eran copias perfectas, el estudio prueba que es posible reducir las cicatrices y mejorar la curación de una forma nunca vista.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean mejorar y hacer más seguro este tratamiento para humanos. Como BMP2 ya está aprobado para algunos usos médicos y FGF2 está en ensayos clínicos, esta terapia podría probarse en personas antes de lo esperado. La investigación también abre nuevas preguntas sobre cómo controlar la sanación y regeneración en mamíferos.

**Frase clave** – Los investigadores encontraron una forma de activar poderes regenerativos ocultos en mamíferos, permitiendo que partes dañadas del cuerpo vuelvan a crecer en lugar de formar cicatrices.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing, partially healed animal limb surrounded by delicate, intertwining strands symbolizing regenerating bone, ligaments, and tendons, with subtle abstract shapes representing growth factors gently guiding the transformation from scar tissue to new, living structures, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones.

articles.title: New procedure delivers lasting knee arthritis pain relief without surgery
articles.summary: A minimally invasive treatment that blocks inflammation-driving blood vessels in the knee provided significant pain relief and improved function for osteoarthritis patients, with benefits lasting at least a year. The procedure was safe, highly successful, and could offer a new alternative for people seeking relief before considering knee replacement.
articles.content:
A minimally invasive procedure that blocks abnormal blood vessels around the knee may offer long lasting relief for people with osteoarthritis, according to new research published on June 16 in Radiology, a journal of the Radiological Society of North America (RSNA).

Osteoarthritis is the most common type of arthritis and is a major cause of disability worldwide. The condition can lead to joint inflammation, stiffness, reduced mobility, and pain caused by sensory nerves. The World Health Organization estimates that more than 365 million adults around the world live with knee osteoarthritis.

"For many patients with knee osteoarthritis, there is a real treatment gap today," said Florian Nima Fleckenstein, M.D., deputy head of Interventional Radiology Campus Mitte, Charité -- Universitätsmedizin Berlin. "Conservative measures such as intra-articular injections no longer provide sufficient relief, but joint replacement is not an option for medical or personal reasons."

A New Approach to Knee Osteoarthritis Pain

The treatment, known as genicular artery embolization (GAE), is an emerging nonsurgical procedure designed to target abnormal blood vessels that develop around arthritic knee joints. These excess vessels are believed to contribute to ongoing inflammation and pain.

During the procedure, an interventional radiologist uses a thin catheter to reach the affected blood vessels and injects tiny particles that block blood flow to them. By shutting down these abnormal vessels, the treatment aims to reduce inflammation and relieve pain without surgery.

For this study, researchers evaluated GAE performed with rapidly resorbable, gelatin-based microspheres. These specially designed spherical particles are calibrated by size and dissolve within hours after being delivered. The team proposed that the material could combine some of the advantages of both temporary and permanent embolization agents while avoiding some of their drawbacks.

"GAE is a whole new treatment regimen that targets abnormal hypervascularity around the joint and, in turn, modulates the pathological neurovascular environment," Dr. Fleckenstein said. "By reducing both inflammation and pain, GAE with resorbable microspheres may be the first procedure that alters the course of the disease, slowing its progression."

Nearly 200 Patients Studied

The prospective single-center study enrolled 194 patients with osteoarthritis-related knee pain, including 114 women and 80 men. All participants had failed to achieve adequate relief after at least three months of conservative treatment, including physiotherapy, anti-inflammatory medications, and intra-articular injections.

The participants had a median age of 69 years and a median body mass index of 28.4.

"We believe these results carry real weight because they come from real-world data," Dr. Fleckenstein said. "With this broad, inclusive study design, our participants are exactly the patients that physicians encounter every day in their practices."

All patients underwent GAE using the resorbable microspheres between July and November 2024. Forty-five participants (23%) received treatment in both knees, with the second procedure performed within four weeks of the first.

In total, researchers carried out 239 GAE procedures. Treatments were performed using image (fluoroscopic) guidance. Every procedure was technically successful. No moderate or severe adverse events occurred, and only 6.7% of participants experienced mild reactions that resolved on their own.

Significant Pain Relief and Improved Mobility

Researchers assessed outcomes before treatment and again at six weeks, three months, six months, and 12 months afterward. The six-month evaluation was conducted in person by an orthopedic surgeon.

Follow-up rates remained high throughout the study, reaching 94% at six weeks (183/194), 89% at three months (172/194), 89% at six months (171/194), and 79% at 12 months (154/194).

"In our cohort, we saw a significant drop in pain and a significant increase in function, including sports and recreation and daily activity," Dr. Fleckenstein said. "Most importantly, their quality of life significantly increased."

Pain levels improved quickly and continued to improve over time. On the Numeric Rating Scale (a 0-to-10 measure of pain intensity), the median score fell from 7 before treatment to 4 after six weeks and then to 3 at both the 6-, and 12-month follow-ups, indicating sustained relief throughout the year.

Patients also showed improvement across every category of the Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score.

Median scores for daily activities increased from 53 to 71.5, while sports and recreation scores rose from 15 to 36. Osteoarthritis-related symptoms improved from 51 to 68. Pain scores increased from 44 to 65 (where 0 indicates extreme knee pain and 100 indicates no pain at all). Quality of life scores improved from 19 to 40.

Most Patients Experienced Meaningful Improvement

Previous research has shown that a reduction in a Numeric Rating Scale pain score of ≥ 2.0 points and an increase of ≥ 10 points in the Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score sub-scores represent clinically meaningful improvements, known as a minimum clinically important difference.

At the 12-month follow-up, 80% of participants exceeded that threshold based on their Numeric Rating Scale pain scores.

"Our study demonstrates that GAE using rapidly resorbable gelatin-based microspheres is a safe, minimally invasive therapy that provides meaningful pain relief and functional improvement in participants with osteoarthritis-related knee symptoms for at least 12 months," Dr. Fleckenstein said. "By embolizing the pathological vessels, we're able to normalize the vessel structure -- and, in turn, the neuronal structure of the knee."

According to Dr. Fleckenstein, the study represents the largest body of evidence so far examining GAE with rapidly resorbable microspheres, involving nearly 200 patients.

"This lets us speak about safety and efficacy with real confidence," he said. "For the right patient, it can mean lasting relief from a single, minimally invasive procedure -- a meaningful new option between injections and joint replacement."

The study, "Genicular Artery Embolization Using Rapidly Resorbable Gelatin-based Microspheres for Osteoarthritis-related Knee Pain," was conducted by Dr. Fleckenstein and colleagues Dina David, M.S., Paolo Garducci, M.D., Tazio Maleitzke, M.D., Stephan Oehme, M.D., Lynn Jeanette Savic, M.D., Timo Alexander Auer, M.D., Bernhard Gebauer, M.D., Tobias Winkler, M.D., Ph.D., and Federico Collettini, M.D.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a new minimally invasive medical procedure that provides significant, lasting pain relief and improved function for a large population of osteoarthritis patients worldwide. The treatment offers a safe alternative to surgery with broad potential impact on quality of life, supported by a substantial clinical study with nearly 200 patients and detailed outcome data.
articles.personality_title: New minimally invasive procedure eases knee arthritis pain for at least a year
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Osteoarthritis is a common joint disease that causes pain, stiffness, and difficulty moving. It affects millions of people worldwide, especially in the knees. Many patients find that medicines and injections stop working, but surgery may not be an option.

**What happened** – Researchers tested a new treatment called genicular artery embolization (GAE) on 194 patients with knee osteoarthritis. This procedure blocks abnormal blood vessels that cause inflammation and pain. Doctors used tiny particles that dissolve after a few hours to safely stop blood flow to these vessels. The treatment was done between July and November 2024 and guided by imaging.

**Impact** – The procedure was successful for all patients and caused only mild, temporary side effects in a few cases. After treatment, most patients felt much less pain and were able to move better. Pain scores dropped from 7 to 3 out of 10, and patients improved in daily activities and sports. These benefits lasted for at least one year, showing that GAE can be a safe, effective alternative to surgery.

**What's next step** – Doctors hope to offer this treatment more widely as a middle option before knee replacement surgery. Further studies may help understand how GAE slows the disease and which patients benefit the most.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new, simple procedure safely reduces knee arthritis pain and improves movement for at least a year without surgery.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle procédure peu invasive soulage la douleur de l’arthrose du genou pendant au moins un an
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'arthrose est une maladie articulaire fréquente qui cause douleur, raideur et difficultés de mouvement. Elle touche des millions de personnes dans le monde, surtout au niveau des genoux. Beaucoup de patients constatent que les médicaments et injections ne suffisent plus, mais la chirurgie n’est pas toujours possible.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont testé un nouveau traitement appelé embolisation de l'artère géniculaire (EAG) sur 194 patients souffrant d'arthrose du genou. Cette procédure bloque les vaisseaux sanguins anormaux qui provoquent inflammation et douleur. Les médecins ont utilisé de petites particules qui se dissolvent en quelques heures pour stopper en toute sécurité le flux sanguin vers ces vaisseaux. Le traitement a eu lieu entre juillet et novembre 2024, guidé par imagerie.

**Impact** – La procédure a réussi pour tous les patients et n’a causé que des effets secondaires légers et temporaires chez quelques-uns. Après le traitement, la plupart des patients ont ressenti beaucoup moins de douleur et ont pu mieux bouger. Les scores de douleur sont passés de 7 à 3 sur 10, et les patients ont amélioré leurs activités quotidiennes et sportives. Ces bénéfices ont duré au moins un an, montrant que l’EAG peut être une alternative sûre et efficace à la chirurgie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les médecins espèrent proposer ce traitement plus largement comme option intermédiaire avant la chirurgie de remplacement du genou. D’autres études pourraient aider à comprendre comment l’EAG ralentit la maladie et quels patients en bénéficient le plus.

**Message clé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle procédure simple réduit en toute sécurité la douleur de l’arthrose du genou et améliore le mouvement pendant au moins un an sans chirurgie.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo procedimiento poco invasivo alivia el dolor de la artritis de rodilla durante al menos un año
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La osteoartritis es una enfermedad común de las articulaciones que causa dolor, rigidez y dificultad para moverse. Afecta a millones de personas en todo el mundo, especialmente en las rodillas. Muchos pacientes descubren que los medicamentos y las inyecciones ya no funcionan, pero la cirugía no siempre es una opción.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores probaron un nuevo tratamiento llamado embolización de la arteria genicular (EAG) en 194 pacientes con osteoartritis de rodilla. Este procedimiento bloquea los vasos sanguíneos anormales que causan inflamación y dolor. Los médicos usaron pequeñas partículas que se disuelven en pocas horas para detener de manera segura el flujo sanguíneo hacia esos vasos. El tratamiento se realizó entre julio y noviembre de 2024, guiado por imágenes.

**Impacto** – El procedimiento fue exitoso en todos los pacientes y causó solo efectos secundarios leves y temporales en algunos casos. Después del tratamiento, la mayoría sintió mucho menos dolor y pudo moverse mejor. Las puntuaciones de dolor bajaron de 7 a 3 sobre 10, y los pacientes mejoraron en actividades diarias y deportes. Estos beneficios duraron al menos un año, mostrando que la EAG puede ser una alternativa segura y eficaz a la cirugía.

**Próximo paso** – Los médicos esperan ofrecer este tratamiento de forma más amplia como opción intermedia antes de la cirugía de reemplazo de rodilla. Nuevos estudios podrían ayudar a entender cómo la EAG ralentiza la enfermedad y qué pacientes se benefician más.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un nuevo procedimiento sencillo reduce de forma segura el dolor de la artritis de rodilla y mejora el movimiento durante al menos un año sin cirugía.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a stylized human knee joint surrounded by delicate, glowing blood vessels gently being blocked by soft, translucent gelatin spheres, set against a calm, natural background with muted earth tones, symbolizing healing and lasting relief through a minimally invasive medical procedure.

articles.title: Towards Conversational AI for Disease Management
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 17 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10764-5Towards Conversational AI for Disease Management
articles.content: While large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in diagnostic dialogue1, their capabilities for effective management reasoning—including disease progression, therapeutic response, and safe medication prescription—remain under-explored. We advance the previously demonstrated diagnostic capabilities of the Articulate Medical Intelligence Explorer (AMIE)1−3 through a new LLM-based agentic system optimized for multi-visit clinical management and dialogue. To ground its reasoning in authoritative clinical knowledge, AMIE leverages Gemini’s long-context capabilities4, combining in-context retrieval with structured reasoning to align its output with up-to-date clinical practice guidelines and drug formularies. In a randomized, blinded virtual Objective Structured Clinical Examination (OSCE) study, AMIE was compared to 21 primary care physicians (PCPs) across 100 multi-visit case scenarios designed to reflect UK NICE Guidance and BMJ Best Practice guidelines. AMIE was non-inferior to PCPs in management reasoning as assessed by specialists and scored better in both preciseness of treatments and investigations, and in its alignment with and grounding in clinical guidelines. To benchmark medication reasoning, we developed RxQA, a multiple-choice question benchmark derived from two national drug formularies (US, UK) and validated by board-certified pharmacists. Though AMIE and PCPs both benefited from the ability to access external drug information, AMIE outperformed PCPs on higher difficulty questions. While further research would be needed before real-world translation, AMIE’s strong performance across evaluations marks a significant step towards conversational AI as a tool in disease management.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant advancement in conversational AI for disease management, demonstrating that the AI system AMIE performs on par or better than primary care physicians in clinical management reasoning and medication prescription. This breakthrough has broad implications for improving healthcare delivery and patient outcomes, supported by rigorous evaluation and alignment with clinical guidelines.
articles.personality_title: New AI matches doctors in managing diseases and prescribing medicines
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Doctors often need to manage diseases over many visits, deciding how illnesses change and which treatments work best. Artificial intelligence (AI) has helped with diagnosing diseases but hasn’t been tested much in managing ongoing care or safely prescribing medicines.

**What happened** – Scientists created a new AI system called AMIE that uses advanced language technology to talk with patients and help doctors manage diseases over time. AMIE was tested in a study where it handled 100 pretend patient cases, compared to 21 real primary care doctors in the UK. Experts checked how well AMIE made treatment decisions and followed official medical guidelines.

**Impact** – AMIE did just as well as the doctors in managing diseases and was even better at choosing precise treatments and following rules about medicines. It also answered tough questions about drugs more accurately than the doctors. This shows AI can help with complicated medical decisions, not just diagnosis.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to do more studies to see how AMIE works in real hospitals and clinics. If successful, this AI could support doctors by providing reliable advice during patient visits, improving care and safety.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The AI system AMIE performs as well as doctors in managing diseases and prescribing medicines, marking a big step for AI in healthcare.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle IA égale les médecins dans la gestion des maladies et la prescription de médicaments
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les médecins doivent souvent gérer des maladies sur plusieurs rendez-vous, en suivant l’évolution des maladies et en choisissant les meilleurs traitements. L’intelligence artificielle (IA) aide déjà au diagnostic, mais son rôle dans le suivi des soins et la prescription sécurisée reste peu exploré.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont créé un nouveau système d’IA appelé AMIE, capable de dialoguer avec les patients et d’aider à gérer les maladies sur le long terme. AMIE a été testé dans une étude avec 100 cas fictifs, comparé à 21 médecins généralistes au Royaume-Uni. Des experts ont évalué la qualité des décisions de traitement et le respect des directives médicales officielles.

**Impact** – AMIE a obtenu des résultats équivalents à ceux des médecins pour la gestion des maladies et a été meilleur pour choisir des traitements précis et suivre les règles sur les médicaments. Il a aussi mieux répondu aux questions difficiles sur les médicaments. Cela montre que l’IA peut aider à prendre des décisions médicales complexes, pas seulement poser des diagnostics.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent tester AMIE dans des hôpitaux et cliniques réels. Si les résultats sont positifs, cette IA pourrait aider les médecins en fournissant des conseils fiables pendant les consultations, améliorant ainsi les soins et la sécurité.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le système d’IA AMIE rivalise avec les médecins pour gérer les maladies et prescrire des médicaments, une avancée majeure pour l’IA en santé.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva IA iguala a médicos en manejo de enfermedades y prescripción de medicamentos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los médicos suelen manejar enfermedades durante varias visitas, observando cómo cambian y eligiendo los mejores tratamientos. La inteligencia artificial (IA) ha ayudado en diagnósticos, pero no se ha probado mucho en el manejo continuo de la atención o en la prescripción segura de medicamentos.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos crearon un nuevo sistema de IA llamado AMIE, que usa tecnología avanzada para conversar con pacientes y ayudar a manejar enfermedades a lo largo del tiempo. AMIE fue probado en un estudio con 100 casos simulados, comparado con 21 médicos de atención primaria en el Reino Unido. Expertos evaluaron qué tan bien AMIE tomaba decisiones de tratamiento y seguía las guías médicas oficiales.

**Impacto** – AMIE tuvo un desempeño igual al de los médicos en el manejo de enfermedades y fue mejor eligiendo tratamientos precisos y siguiendo reglas sobre medicamentos. También respondió mejor preguntas difíciles sobre fármacos. Esto muestra que la IA puede ayudar en decisiones médicas complejas, no solo en diagnósticos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren hacer más estudios para ver cómo funciona AMIE en hospitales y clínicas reales. Si tiene éxito, esta IA podría apoyar a los médicos dando consejos confiables durante las consultas, mejorando la atención y la seguridad.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El sistema de IA AMIE iguala a los médicos en manejar enfermedades y prescribir medicamentos, un avance importante para la IA en la salud.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an open medical book glowing softly on a wooden desk, surrounded by interconnected glowing threads symbolizing complex clinical knowledge, with a stylized, abstract representation of a thoughtful AI brain made of light and circuitry gently guiding a series of transparent patient visit silhouettes arranged like pages turning in a calendar.
